properties: The result is entirely lowercase.
Every space character and every underscore character is treated as a word separator and replaced by a hyphen.
Consecutive separators (spaces and/or underscores in any combination) are collapsed into a single hyphen.
Any character that is not a letter, digit, space, or underscore is stripped (removed) from the output.
The result contains only lowercase letters, digits, and hyphens.
No leading or trailing hyphens appear in the result unless the input consists entirely of separators.
edge_cases: Multiple consecutive spaces collapse to one hyphen: 'a  b' -> 'a-b'.
Mixed spaces and underscores collapse to one hyphen: 'a_ b' -> 'a-b'.
Punctuation such as '!', ',', '.' is stripped entirely, not replaced with a hyphen.
An underscore adjacent to a space counts as a single separator group: 'A_b  c!' -> 'a-b-c'.
Empty string input returns empty string.
Input with only punctuation/separators may return empty string or a single hyphen depending on content.

properties: Each character group in the output consists of the character followed immediately by its count as a decimal integer.
Groups appear in the same left-to-right order as the corresponding runs in the input string.
Every character in the input is accounted for: the sum of all counts in the output equals len(s).
An empty input string produces an empty output string.
Consecutive identical characters are merged into a single group; no two adjacent groups share the same character.
edge_cases: Empty string must return empty string, not raise an error.
A single character returns that character followed by '1'.
All identical characters produce one group with count equal to len(s).
Alternating characters each produce individual groups with count 1.
The count is always present even when it equals 1 (e.g., 'b' -> 'b1', not 'b').

contract_obj.signature: def is_anagram(a: str, b: str) -> bool
properties: Returns True if and only if the two strings contain exactly the same characters with the same frequencies after converting both to lowercase and removing all space characters.
Comparison is case-insensitive: uppercase and lowercase versions of the same letter are treated as identical.
All space characters (ASCII 0x20) are ignored and do not affect the result.
Returns False when the sorted, cleaned character sequences of a and b differ in any way.
The function is symmetric: is_anagram(a, b) == is_anagram(b, a) for all inputs.
edge_cases: Both strings empty after stripping spaces: returns True.
One string empty and the other non-empty (after stripping spaces): returns False.
Strings that are identical after lowercasing and space removal: returns True.
Strings with different lengths after space removal always return False.
Strings differing only in case (e.g., 'ABC' vs 'abc') return True.
Strings containing only spaces (e.g., '   ' vs '') are treated as empty and return True.
Duplicate characters must match in frequency, not just presence (e.g., 'aab' vs 'abb' returns False).

properties: For a list with an odd number of elements, returns the middle value of the sorted list as its original numeric type.
For a list with an even number of elements, returns the arithmetic mean of the two middle values of the sorted list, which may be a float.
The input list xs must be non-empty; behavior for empty lists is undefined.
The function does not modify the original list.
The returned value equals the value at index len(xs)//2 of the sorted list when len(xs) is odd.
The returned value equals (sorted_xs[n//2 - 1] + sorted_xs[n//2]) / 2 when len(xs) is even, where n = len(xs).
edge_cases: Single-element list returns that element unchanged.
Two-element list returns the average of both elements as a float.
List already sorted in descending order must still yield correct median after internal sorting.
List with duplicate values is handled correctly (e.g., [2, 2, 2] returns 2).
List with negative numbers (e.g., [-3, -1, -2] returns -2).
Mixed negative and positive numbers (e.g., [-1, 0, 1] returns 0).

properties: Input n must be an integer in the closed range [1, 50].
The returned string uses only the characters I, V, X, L and follows standard subtractive Roman numeral notation.
4 is represented as 'IV' (subtractive), never 'IIII'.
9 is represented as 'IX' (subtractive), never 'VIIII'.
40 is represented as 'XL' (subtractive), never 'XXXX'.
The result is the unique, canonical Roman numeral string for n using the standard subtractive pairs: IV=4, IX=9, XL=40.
The returned string is non-empty for all valid inputs.
Characters appear in non-increasing value order (largest symbols first, with subtractive pairs treated as single units).
edge_cases: n=1 is the minimum valid input and returns 'I'.
n=50 is the maximum valid input and returns 'L'.
n=4 must return 'IV', not 'IIII'.
n=9 must return 'IX', not 'VIIII'.
n=40 must return 'XL', not 'XXXX'.
n=49 must return 'XLIX' (combining both XL and IX subtractive pairs).
n=39 must return 'XXXIX' (three X's followed by IX).

properties: The output is always a flat list containing no nested lists.
Every non-list element from the input, at any depth of nesting, appears in the output exactly once.
The relative order of all non-list elements is preserved from left-to-right depth-first traversal of the input.
The return type is always a list.
If the input is an empty list, the output is an empty list.
edge_cases: Empty list input returns empty list.
Already flat list returns a list equal to the input.
Arbitrarily deep nesting (e.g., 5+ levels) must be fully flattened.
Mixed nesting depths within the same list are handled correctly.
A list containing only nested empty lists returns an empty list.
Non-list elements (integers, strings, etc.) are treated as atomic and passed through unchanged.

properties: Returns the total count of characters in s that are vowels (a, e, i, o, u), matched case-insensitively.
The count is a non-negative integer.
Each character in s is checked independently; only characters whose lowercase form is in {'a','e','i','o','u'} are counted.
The original string s is not modified.
edge_cases: Empty string returns 0.
String with no vowels (e.g., 'rhythm', 'bcdfg') returns 0.
String with all vowels in mixed case (e.g., 'AeIoU') returns the full length.
Non-alphabetic characters (digits, punctuation, spaces) are never counted as vowels.
Uppercase vowels (A, E, I, O, U) are counted the same as their lowercase equivalents.
String consisting of a single vowel returns 1.
String consisting of a single non-vowel returns 0.

properties: Returns x unchanged if lo <= x <= hi.
Returns hi if x > hi.
Returns lo if x < lo.
The returned value is always in the closed interval [lo, hi].
edge_cases: x equals lo exactly: returns lo.
x equals hi exactly: returns hi.
x is between lo and hi (exclusive): returns x unmodified.
x is greater than hi: returns hi, not x.
x is less than lo: returns lo, not x.

properties: The returned list contains only the first occurrence of each element from xs, preserving original order.
Every element in the returned list appears exactly once.
Every element in the returned list was present in xs.
The relative order of first occurrences in the returned list matches their order in xs.
len(returned list) equals the number of distinct elements in xs.
No element from xs is omitted from the returned list if it has not appeared earlier in xs.
edge_cases: Empty list returns empty list.
List with all identical elements returns a single-element list.
List with no duplicates returns a list equal to the input.
Elements are compared by equality (==), so 1 and True may be considered duplicates.
The input list is not modified.

properties: Every lowercase letter in s is shifted forward by k positions within the lowercase alphabet (a-z) with wraparound, preserving lowercase case.
Every uppercase letter in s is shifted forward by k positions within the uppercase alphabet (A-Z) with wraparound, preserving uppercase case.
Every non-letter character (spaces, punctuation, digits, etc.) is left completely unchanged in its original position.
The output string has exactly the same length as the input string.
The shift k wraps around modulo 26, so shifting 'z' by 1 yields 'a' and shifting 'Z' by 1 yields 'A'.
edge_cases: k=0 returns the string unchanged.
k=26 returns the string unchanged (full wraparound).
Shifting 'z' or 'Z' by 1 wraps to 'a' or 'A' respectively.
A string with only non-letter characters is returned as-is.
An empty string returns an empty string.
k larger than 26 is handled via modulo 26 (e.g., k=27 behaves like k=1).
Mixed case strings preserve the case of each individual letter independently.

properties: The return value is a list of lists where every inner list except possibly the last has exactly n elements.
The last inner list contains the remaining elements and has between 1 and n elements inclusive (when xs is non-empty).
Concatenating all inner lists in order produces a list equal to xs.
The total number of inner lists is ceil(len(xs) / n) when xs is non-empty, and 0 when xs is empty.
The relative order of elements is preserved across all chunks.
edge_cases: Empty list xs returns an empty list regardless of n.
When len(xs) is exactly divisible by n, no partial chunk exists at the end.
When n is larger than len(xs), a single chunk containing all elements is returned.
When n equals 1, each element is wrapped in its own single-element list.
The function does not mutate the input list xs.

properties: Returns True if and only if every opening bracket '(', '[', '{' is closed by its corresponding closing bracket ')', ']', '}' in correct nesting order.
A closing bracket must match the most recently opened unmatched opening bracket (LIFO/stack discipline).
Returns True for an empty string.
Returns False if any closing bracket does not match the top of the pending-open stack.
Returns False if the string ends with any unmatched opening brackets remaining.
Non-bracket characters, if present, are ignored and do not affect the result.
edge_cases: Empty string returns True.
Single opening bracket with no closing bracket returns False.
Single closing bracket with no opening bracket returns False.
Interleaved mismatched pairs like '([)]' return False even though bracket counts are equal.
Deeply nested but correctly matched brackets return True.
Multiple separate balanced groups like '()[]{}' return True.

properties: Returns the single word (lowercased) that appears most frequently when text is split on whitespace.
Words are compared case-insensitively by converting each token to lowercase before counting.
If multiple words share the highest frequency, any one of them may be returned (tie-breaking is unspecified).
The returned value is always a lowercase string.

args: a b a c a b
the cat sat on the mat the

edge_cases: Single word input returns that word lowercased.
All words appear equally often — any of the tied words may be returned.
Mixed-case tokens (e.g. 'Hello', 'HELLO') are folded to lowercase and counted together.
Extra whitespace between words (multiple spaces, tabs) is handled by standard split() semantics — empty tokens are not counted.
Input consisting of a single repeated word returns that word.

properties: Every uppercase letter that is preceded by a lowercase letter or digit is replaced by an underscore followed by the lowercase version of that letter.

Every uppercase letter that is preceded by another uppercase letter and followed by a lowercase letter is replaced by an underscore followed by the lowercase version of that letter (handles sequences like 'XMLParser' -> 'xml_parser').
The returned string contains only lowercase letters, digits, and underscores.
No leading or trailing underscores are introduced by the conversion of a string that does not begin or end with an uppercase letter boundary.
The function returns a new string and does not mutate the input.
edge_cases: Empty string input returns empty string.
Single uppercase letter returns single lowercase letter with no underscore.
All uppercase string (e.g. 'ABC') returns all lowercase with no underscores between consecutive uppercase letters that form an acronym at the end.
String already in snake_case is returned unchanged (no double underscores introduced).
Digits in the string are treated as non-uppercase characters and do not trigger underscore insertion before them.

properties: Returns the greatest common divisor of all integers in xs, i.e., the largest positive integer that divides every element of xs without remainder.
The result divides every element in xs evenly (result % x == 0 for all x in xs).
No integer larger than the result divides every element in xs.
The result is always a positive integer.
The function is commutative with respect to list order: reordering xs does not change the result.
All elements of xs must be positive integers (xs contains at least one element).
edge_cases: Single-element list: gcd_list([n]) == n for any positive integer n.
All elements are equal: gcd_list([k, k, k]) == k.
Pairwise coprime elements (e.g., [3, 5, 7]): result is 1.
List contains 1: result is always 1 since gcd(any, 1) == 1.
Two-element list: behaves identically to standard two-argument gcd.
Large list of the same value: result equals that value.
Elements that are multiples of each other: result equals the smallest element.

contract_obj.signature: pad_center(s: str, width: int, fill: str) -> str
properties: The returned string has length max(len(s), width).
The original string s appears centered within the result, with fill characters padding both sides.
When the total padding needed is odd, the extra fill character is placed on the right side (matching str.center behavior).
If len(s) >= width, the returned string equals s unchanged.
The fill parameter must be a single character string used for padding.
Left padding length equals floor((width - len(s)) / 2) when width > len(s).
Right padding length equals ceil((width - len(s)) / 2) when width > len(s).
edge_cases: When width equals len(s), return s with no padding added.
When width is less than len(s), return s unchanged.
When width is 0 and s is empty, return empty string.
Odd total padding places the extra fill character on the right, not the left.
fill is exactly one character; behavior with multi-character fill is undefined.
s may be an empty string; padding still applies to reach width.
